planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to deep shade; Walter's violet spreads by stolons to form a creeping ground cover mat in shaded woodland settings — one of the most shade-tolerant native violets.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; Walter's violet is a low-growing, creeping native violet of the southeastern Coastal Plain woodland understory, spreading by above-ground stolons to form a dense, attractive mat of small, rounded, mottled-silver leaves. The leaves often have attractive silver or grayish variegation that makes the plant ornamentally distinguished even when not in flower. The small, lavender-blue to pale violet flowers in spring are charming on close inspection. As a creeping, rhizomatous violet, it is one of the most effective native alternatives to invasive ground covers for shaded, moist woodland floors in zone 9a. The larvae of numerous fritillary butterfly species use native violets as their host plant — including the variegated fritillary, gulf fritillary, and great spangled fritillary.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Australian violet spreads vigorously by stolons and runners to form a dense, grass-excluding ground cover mat in cool, moist, shaded conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; Australian violet is a low-growing, spreading violet from the moist, shaded woodlands and gullies of southeastern Australia, producing attractive, kidney-shaped leaves and small, charming violet flowers with white-and-purple markings that bloom nearly year-round in mild climates. It spreads by horizontal stolons to form a dense, low, weed-suppressing mat that makes an excellent ground cover under trees in shade. In zone 9a, it thrives as an evergreen ground cover in moist, shaded areas such as under live oaks and camellias, where it provides continuous year-round ground cover and periodic bloom. It tolerates foot traffic better than most shade ground covers. A popular ground cover in Australian gardens, it has become a favorite for shade gardeners across the Gulf South.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; arrowhead violet is one of the more drought-tolerant native violets and grows in open, dry to moist habitats from meadows to woodland margins throughout the eastern US.

directions: Plant in dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; arrowhead violet is distinguished by its distinctively narrow, arrowhead-shaped leaves with pointed, backward-directed basal lobes — a shape quite unlike the typical rounded or heart-shaped leaves of most violets. The deep violet to blue-violet flowers in spring have the typical viola form. Like all native violets, it is an essential larval host plant for multiple fritillary butterfly species and provides nectar for early-season native bees. The arrowhead leaf shape makes it easy to identify and distinguishes it from the common blue violet (V. sororia). More tolerant of dry, open conditions than many woodland violets — suitable for meadow garden and prairie garden plantings as well as woodland edges.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic, cool, well-drained soil in deep shade; sweet white violet is one of the most shade-tolerant native violets and grows in cool, moist, acidic forest conditions in the Northeast and Appalachians.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic, well-drained soil in deep shade under deciduous trees; sweet white violet is one of the smallest and most shade-tolerant native violets of the eastern woodland, producing tiny, delicate white flowers with purple veining in early spring — often the first violet to bloom in cold-climate woodlands. The small, heart-shaped leaves form attractive, glossy ground cover mats in moist, acidic shade. The plant spreads slowly by rhizomes and produces aboveground stolons in summer to colonize nearby areas. An excellent species for the deep shade of northern woodland gardens where its requirement for cool, moist, acidic conditions is easily met. Like all native violets, it serves as a host plant for multiple fritillary butterfly species. In zone 7 and below only — does not tolerate the heat and humidity of the Gulf South.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; palmate violet grows in dry to moist, open deciduous woodland and forest edge habitats across the eastern US.

directions: Plant in dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; palmate violet is distinguished by its deeply palmately lobed, finger-like leaf divisions — distinctly different from the typical heart or arrow-shaped leaves of most native violets. The leaf shape is variable, with the spring leaves often deeply lobed and the later summer leaves less so. The violet-purple flowers in early spring are typical of the native blue violets. Growing in dry to moderately moist woodland conditions, it is more tolerant of dry shade than the moisture-requiring woodland violets. Like all native violets, it provides larval food for multiple fritillary butterfly species including the great spangled fritillary, Diana fritillary, and regal fritillary. An ecologically important woodland plant for butterfly gardeners.
